top_quote.text: This month, I shall have been in the House 30 years

reasons.rationale: The speaker uses comparative international examples (Turkey, India, Muslim women) to argue that Palestinian women should be granted political voting rights on equal terms as men.

quotes.text: whether I am correctly informed that all the Jewish councils, when questioned as to the proposed municipal ordinance, expressed their willingness to see women vote on the same terms as men
in Turkey and in India Moslem women are permitted to enjoy political rights

reasons.rationale: The speaker invokes the precedent of Muslim women in Turkey and India having political rights to argue that the British government should extend similar rights in Palestine.

quotes.text: bring them at least up to the level of those in Oriental countries

top_quote.text: expressed their willingness to see women vote on the same terms as men

reasons.rationale: The speaker discusses women's sickness rates and insurance benefits in the context of social insurance policy, not women's political rights or representation.

quotes.text: the implication which is being drawn by the general public that women in general, and married women in particular, are malingerers
the sickness which follows on pregnancy and arises out of labour

top_quote.text: the implication which is being drawn by the general public that women in general, and married women in particular, are malingerers

reasons.rationale: The TARGET speaker (Balfour) argues against the Bill primarily on grounds of parliamentary inefficiency and confusion caused by multiple overlapping electoral reform bills being sent to different committees simultaneously.

quotes.text: the discussion of the same subjects upstairs and downstairs at the same time is a great waste of Parliamentary energy
tinkering with this subject, and then on the top of it all, not bringing them before the same Grand Committee

top_quote.text: the discussion of the same subjects upstairs and downstairs at the same time is a great waste of Parliamentary energy

reasons.rationale: The speaker highlights that women in Yugoslavia voted for the first time in the country's history as evidence of democratic progress and expanded political rights.

quotes.text: for the first time in the history of Yugoslavia, the women exercised the right to vote
one woman over 100 years old, a peasant, said she had lived her life to see the day when she could exercise the right to vote

reasons.rationale: The speaker uses the expansion of the electorate from 3.8 million to 8 million as evidence that enfranchising women contributed to genuine democratic growth.

quotes.text: the number of the electorate was raised from 3,829,000 to 8,020,000
Would one suggest, with figures of that description, that democracy was not being promoted?

top_quote.text: for the first time in the history of Yugoslavia, the women exercised the right to vote

reasons.rationale: The speaker argues explicitly for equal representation of women in the Scottish Parliament, citing the goal of 50:50 gender balance and the legacy of the suffrage movement.

quotes.text: we must do all we can to achieve equal representation in our 21st-century Scottish Parliament
the principle that there should be an equal number of men and women as members of the first Scottish Parliament

reasons.rationale: The speaker invokes the historical legacy of the suffragette movement and the 1928 Equal Franchise Act as foundations and inspiration for continuing to advance women's political representation.

quotes.text: They made it possible for us women to be here today
the 1928 Act was not the final word

reasons.rationale: The speaker criticises the Liberal Democrats' inaction as ensuring the Scottish Parliament will be male-dominated and fail to reflect its electorate, arguing positive party action is needed to deliver real representation.

quotes.text: the new Scottish Parliament will not reflect the people who vote for it. It will be a male-dominated body after all
one female Member of Parliament in Scotland in 75 years of women being elected to Parliament

top_quote.text: we must do all we can to achieve equal representation in our 21st-century Scottish Parliament

reasons.rationale: The speaker argues that women's domestic contributions have economic value equivalent to men's taxable income, implying women deserve equal recognition and rights in family allowance legislation.

quotes.text: assess the services of the wife in the home in terms of money
the mother should receive the allowance

reasons.rationale: The speaker critiques the Bill's original form as effectively an 'endowment of fatherhood' rather than supporting mothers, and praises the free vote as a pragmatic concession to women's interests.

quotes.text: it was really a Bill which would in fact be an endowment of fatherhood
he would have become the most unpopular Minister in the country with the women

reasons.rationale: The speaker highlights the democratic deficit in that 601 men in the House will determine women's entitlements, implicitly arguing for greater women's political representation.

quotes.text: 601 men in the House will determine whether the mother should receive the allowance
the women Member are very generous in welcoming a free vote

top_quote.text: 601 men in the House will determine whether the mother should receive the allowance

reasons.rationale: The speaker argues that women are subjected to these Acts precisely because they lack the vote, making this a direct political rights argument about women's disenfranchisement enabling their legal oppression.

quotes.text: Women were obliged to submit because they had no votes.
The law, as it stood, encouraged men to think that they might, for their own purposes, oppress women as they would.

reasons.rationale: The speaker contends that men would never endure the same treatment women are subjected to, highlighting a gendered double standard enforced by power rather than law.

quotes.text: this was a system which he contended would never be endured by the men of this country
no hon. Member would dare to introduce a measure into that House for the purpose of applying it to men

reasons.rationale: The speaker frames the Acts as morally wrong by arguing that women are branded and degraded by legislation that treats them all alike without regard to their circumstances, denying them dignity.

quotes.text: Once a woman had the misfortune to fall under the notice of a policeman there was no help for her; the law branded her with the mark of Cain
the treatment in hospitals had an hardening effect, because the law treated all alike

top_quote.text: Women were obliged to submit because they had no votes.

reasons.rationale: The speaker argues the immigration rules formally and intentionally discriminate against women by denying them the same right as men to bring foreign spouses into the country.

quotes.text: the rules discriminate against women. The discrimination is formal, intentional and undeniable.
fundamental distinction between the treatment of women resident in this country and that of men who enjoy the same condition

reasons.rationale: The speaker criticises the rules for failing to grant all British women the genuine right to live with their foreign husbands, noting that genuine marriages will be kept separate.

quotes.text: many partners in genuine marriages will be kept separate
all British women, irrespective of how they became British, whether by birth or by registration, shall be granted the right to bring their husbands into the country

reasons.rationale: The speaker exposes the political manoeuvring behind the rules, arguing the Home Secretary crafted a compromise that only superficially liberalises women's rights to satisfy the European Court of Human Rights while appeasing Conservative backbenchers.

quotes.text: the Home Secretary will be able to proclaim his liberalism to the European Court of Human Rights
compact that has been achieved between the Home Secretary and his Back Benchers

top_quote.text: the rules discriminate against women. The discrimination is formal, intentional and undeniable.

reasons.rationale: The speaker points to the Welsh Assembly and Scottish Parliament as successful examples where positive mechanisms (e.g., twinning/zipping) produced high levels of women's representation, including more women than men in the Welsh Cabinet.

quotes.text: the Welsh Assembly and the Scottish Parliament have some of the best representation of women in Europe
there are more women than men in the Cabinet of the Welsh Assembly

reasons.rationale: The speaker frames the use of positive mechanisms by a political party as a means of achieving gender equality in political representation.

quotes.text: A positive mechanism was used by a political party to ensure that the Welsh Assembly and the Scottish Parliament

top_quote.text: the Welsh Assembly and the Scottish Parliament have some of the best representation of women in Europe

reasons.rationale: The speaker argues it would be unfair to women to deny them employment opportunities, and that trade unions have not been fair to women, implying women deserve equal treatment.

quotes.text: it would be unfair to women if by voting against this you keep some of them out of employment
the trade unions are not fair to the women in many ways

reasons.rationale: The speaker anticipates that young women will gain the vote (at 21) within five years, and that once enfranchised, Parliament will have to take their views more seriously.

quotes.text: by that time, I hope, the young women will be 21, and have the vote
hon. Members will have to listen to them more attentively than now

reasons.rationale: The speaker notes the proposal is experimental and only for five years, suggesting pragmatic support contingent on it not being used to exploit women as cheap labour.

quotes.text: It is experimental, and only for five years.
if it really means that this is going to open the way to using women as cheap labour, it would be wrong

top_quote.text: by that time, I hope, the young women will be 21, and have the vote

reasons.rationale: The TARGET speech is entirely focused on the mechanics and economics of unemployment insurance, specifically flat-rate contributions versus wage-proportional systems, and employer contributions — with no reference to women's political rights or representation.

quotes.text: the British method of flat rates of contributions and flat rates of benefit
the employer's contribution is always adhered to

reasons.rationale: The speaker argues that women's interests as voters must be considered in the timing of the by-election, and that the House struggles to do so because it is dominated by men.

quotes.text: I believe that in the constituency of Richmond, Yorks there are people whose interests would be disadvantaged by an early election—the female electorate
It is difficult for this House to consider women's interests because the vast majority of hon. Members—through no fault of their own—are men

reasons.rationale: The speaker calls on the House to overcome its male-majority disadvantage and consider women's perspectives in political decision-making.

quotes.text: They must try to overcome that disadvantage and look at matters through our eyes now and again

reasons.rationale: The speaker opposes an industrial relations order imposed on Northern Ireland, arguing it weakens protections for workers, particularly part-time workers, without local justification.

It puts into north of Ireland legislation the anti-union, anti-worker programme

top_quote.text: part-time workers—who, in the north of Ireland, are almost by definition women—will have their protection substantially weakened

reasons.rationale: The speaker argues that the current electoral system fails to elect a proportionate share of women MEPs, using this as evidence that the system is broken and needs reform.

quotes.text: does it ensure that the right proportion of women are elected as MEPs? It does not.
only two countries in Europe—Italy and Portugal—had a lower proportion of women MEPs elected than Britain

reasons.rationale: The speaker uses the poor representation of women as one of several practical failures of the current system, arguing that the system lacks compensatory benefits and therefore requires fixing.

quotes.text: we do not have other compensating factors

top_quote.text: does it ensure that the right proportion of women are elected as MEPs? It does not.

reasons.rationale: The speaker argues that Labour members are actively voting for women candidates in greater numbers, demonstrating support for women's political representation.

an indication that Labour is voting for women as many candidates came near the top of the list

appointments are made by the Government

reasons.rationale: The speaker draws a direct historical parallel to arguments used against women's suffrage, dismissing the claim that young people don't want the vote just as the same claim was once falsely made about women.

it compelled them to adopt this principle of women suffrage whether it might wreck the Bill or not

reasons.rationale: The TARGET speech is primarily about parliamentary business scheduling (Finance Bill, Scotland Bill, foreign affairs debate, BSE report) with only a passing dismissive remark about equal representation lectures from the Opposition.

reasons.rationale: The speaker argues that women's substantial war service—filling munition factories and replacing men who went to fight—gives them a legitimate and unanswerable claim to enfranchisement if any new franchise is created on grounds of state service.

quotes.text: they presented to me not only a reasonable, but, I think, from their point of view, an unanswerable case

reasons.rationale: The speaker opposes opening the franchise question during wartime, arguing it would distract from the war effort and open floodgates to a multitude of complex competing claims.

quotes.text: the serious menace which would be the result if women had a vote

reasons.rationale: The speaker argues that a change of this magnitude should not be made through a private member's bill on a Friday afternoon, but should first be debated at a general election.
